items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.q: Why are invasive species often more harmful than native species?
items.correct: They lack natural predators or competitors in their new environment, so they spread unchecked.
items.misconception: They are inherently more aggressive than native species.
items.random_wrong: They are larger than native species.
items.why: Students attribute success to species traits. The key factor is the ABSENCE OF CONTROLS - the same species may be ordinary in its native range, where co-evolved predators and competitors keep it in check.

items.q: Why is atmospheric nitrogen unavailable to most plants directly?
items.correct: N2 has a strong triple bond that most organisms cannot break.
items.misconception: Because plants cannot reach high enough to access it.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Despite 78% of air being N2, the molecule's triple bond is energetically costly to break. Only specialised nitrogen-fixing bacteria (and lightning, industrial Haber process) can convert it to usable forms.

items.q: Which is the IUCN Red List category for highest risk of extinction (excluding extinct)?
items.why: Correct answer: Critically Endangered. “Vulnerable” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Simpson's index value close to 1 means:
items.why: It's High diversity. “Low diversity” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many IUCN categories are considered 'threatened' (CR, EN, VU)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
